content.text: Coroutines: The Building Block of Async Python

At the heart of Python's async system is a deceptively simple idea: what if a function could pause itself mid-execution, hand control back to the caller, and resume exactly where it left off later? That idea is a
, and it is the fundamental unit around which everything in Python's

in Python is a special kind of function that can suspend its execution at defined points and later resume from the same point, without blocking the thread it runs on. Unlike a regular function that runs from start to finish in one shot, a coroutine can be paused mid-way — typically while waiting for something slow like a network response or file read — and yield control back to an event loop that can run other tasks in the meantime.
In modern Python (3.5+), coroutines are defined using the

async def greet():
    print("Hello")
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
    print("World")
keyword tells Python: "this is not a regular function — it is a coroutine function." Calling it does
execute it immediately. Instead, it returns a
coro = greet()   # Returns a coroutine object, prints nothing
To actually run it, you must either
it inside another coroutine, or pass it to an event loop:

import asyncio
asyncio.run(greet())   # Actually executes it
Most programs spend a large fraction of their time simply
— waiting for a database query, an HTTP response, a file read, or a timer. During that wait, a traditional synchronous program does nothing useful. It just blocks the thread.
Coroutines solve this by making it possible to

expression, it pauses and hands control back to the event loop. The event loop can then run another coroutine that is ready to make progress. When the original wait completes, the first coroutine is resumed.
This enables programs to handle thousands of concurrent I/O operations on a
— no threads, no processes, no locking, no race conditions.
that handle many simultaneous client connections
that fire many HTTP requests in parallel
like chat servers or live dashboards
High concurrency on a single thread.

Because coroutines cooperatively yield control rather than blocking, a single Python thread can manage thousands of concurrent operations — something that would require thousands of threads in a synchronous model, each consuming memory and scheduling overhead.

Since only one coroutine runs at a time on a thread (cooperative multitasking), there is no shared-state mutation happening simultaneously. This eliminates the most common class of concurrency bugs — race conditions and deadlocks — that plague multi-threaded programs.
A coroutine object in Python is cheap — far cheaper than a thread (which carries a full OS-managed stack). A modern event loop can manage tens of thousands of coroutines simultaneously.

syntax reads almost like synchronous code. The structure of the code mirrors the structure of the logic, which is a significant improvement over callback-based async patterns (such as those in older JavaScript or Twisted Python).
). This predictability makes async code easier to reason about than pre-emptive threading, where a thread switch can happen anywhere.
Coroutines do not make CPU-bound code faster. If a coroutine is doing heavy computation, it blocks the thread and starves other coroutines. For CPU-bound parallelism, Python still needs

Once a piece of code is async, everything that calls it must also be async (or explicitly schedule it). This "async coloring" problem means adopting coroutines in an existing codebase requires significant refactoring.
Not all Python libraries are async-compatible. Standard blocking libraries (like
) block the event loop when called from a coroutine. Async equivalents (
Stack traces in async code can be harder to read. Errors that happen inside awaited coroutines may have their context obscured. Tools like
extensions help, but the learning curve is real.
Coroutines run concurrently (interleaved) but not in parallel (simultaneously). Two coroutines cannot execute at the exact same CPU instant within one event loop.
The Mechanics of Suspension and Resumption
Under the hood, a coroutine is implemented as a
, it transforms the function body into an object that implements the
expression becomes a yield point — a place where the coroutine can suspend and hand a value back to whoever is driving it.
The thing driving coroutines is the
(covered in detail in the Event Loop topic). The event loop keeps a queue of coroutines that are ready to run. When it resumes a coroutine, the coroutine runs until it hits an
. At that point, the coroutine tells the event loop what it is waiting for (a socket becoming readable, a timer expiring, etc.), and the event loop moves on to the next ready coroutine.
When the awaited condition is satisfied (the socket has data, the timer fires), the event loop marks the suspended coroutine as ready again and eventually resumes it from the exact line it paused at.
is the mechanism by which one coroutine yields control. It takes an
object — which is any object that implements
. The three main awaitable types are:
(coroutines wrapped and scheduled by the event loop)
(low-level promise-like objects representing a pending result)

async def fetch_data():
    await asyncio.sleep(2)    # Suspends here for 2 seconds
    return {"key": "value"}

async def main():
    result = await fetch_data()   # Suspends here until fetch_data completes
    print(result)
, it suspends. The event loop begins running
, it also suspends. The event loop registers a timer and is now free to run any other ready coroutines. After 2 seconds, the timer fires,
A coroutine object progresses through distinct states:
function was called, returning a coroutine object. No code has run yet.
— The event loop is actively executing the coroutine's body.
and yielded control. It is waiting for an event.
— The coroutine has returned (or raised an exception). Its result is available.
Once a coroutine completes, it cannot be restarted. If the same operation needs to be repeated, a new coroutine object must be created by calling the
Architecture: Coroutines in the Async Execution Model
The event loop sits at the center. Each coroutine runs until it awaits something — at which point control returns to the loop. The loop monitors all pending I/O and timers at the OS level (using
depending on the platform). When any awaited condition is met, the loop resumes the corresponding coroutine.
The bottleneck is I/O — network calls, file reads, database queries, sub-process communication
Many operations need to run concurrently without the overhead of threads
Building servers, crawlers, API gateways, or real-time data pipelines
The work is CPU-bound (image processing, machine learning inference, cryptography) — use
The codebase is heavily synchronous and refactoring would be costly without clear benefit
Third-party dependencies are blocking-only and have no async equivalent

import asyncio

async def download(url: str) -> str:
    print(f"Starting download: {url}")
    await asyncio.sleep(1)   # Simulates I/O wait
    print(f"Finished download: {url}")
    return f"Data from {url}"
Step 2: Await it from another coroutine

async def main():
    result = await download("https://example.com/file1")
    print(result)
creates a new event loop, runs the given coroutine until it completes, and then closes the loop. It is the standard entry point for async programs in Python 3.7+.
Step 4: Run multiple coroutines concurrently with

async def main():
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        download("https://example.com/file1"),
        download("https://example.com/file2"),
        download("https://example.com/file3"),
    )
    for r in results:
        print(r)
, all three downloads run concurrently — the total time is roughly the time of the slowest single download, not the sum of all three.

async def task(name, delay):
    await asyncio.sleep(delay)
    print(f"{name} done")

async def main():
    await task("A", 1)
    await task("B", 1)
    await task("C", 1)

asyncio.run(main())

async def main():
    await asyncio.gather(
        task("A", 1),
        task("B", 1),
        task("C", 1),
    )

asyncio.run(main())
The concurrent version runs all three tasks interleaved on a single thread. Each task suspends at
, the event loop runs the others, and all three complete in roughly 1 second total.

async def square(n: int) -> int:
    await asyncio.sleep(0)   # Yield control once, simulate async work
    return n * n

async def main():
    a, b, c = await asyncio.gather(square(3), square(4), square(5))
    print(a, b, c)   # 9 16 25

asyncio.run(main())

async def risky():
    await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
    raise ValueError("Something went wrong")

async def main():
    try:
        await risky()
    except ValueError as e:
        print(f"Caught: {e}")

asyncio.run(main())
patterns used in synchronous code work as expected.
Consider a price-checking service that must fetch current prices from 50 different e-commerce APIs every 30 seconds. Each API call takes between 200ms and 800ms.
50 calls × ~500ms average = ~25 seconds per cycle. Far too slow for a 30-second refresh window.

import asyncio
import aiohttp

URLS = [f"https://api.store{i}.com/price/item42" for i in range(1, 51)]

async def fetch_price(session: aiohttp.ClientSession, url: str) -> dict:
    async with session.get(url, timeout=aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=5)) as resp:
        data = await resp.json()
        return {"url": url, "price": data["price"]}

async def fetch_all_prices():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [fetch_price(session, url) for url in URLS]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
    
    for r in results:
        if isinstance(r, Exception):
            print(f"Failed: {r}")
        else:
            print(f"{r['url']}: ${r['price']}")

asyncio.run(fetch_all_prices())

All 50 requests fire nearly simultaneously. The total time is roughly the slowest single request, not the sum of all 50. On a single thread, with no threading complexity, this solution handles the full workload comfortably within the 30-second window. The
ensures one failing API does not cancel all others.
gives back a coroutine object, not a result. The coroutine never actually runs. Python 3.11+ will raise a
warning, but in older versions this bug can be silent.

# Wrong — coro object is created but never executed
result = download("https://example.com")

# Correct
result = await download("https://example.com")
, or any other blocking function inside a coroutine freezes the entire event loop for the duration of that call. No other coroutine can run during that time.

# Wrong — blocks the event loop
async def bad():
    time.sleep(2)        # Blocks everything
    requests.get(url)    # Blocks everything

# Correct
async def good():
    await asyncio.sleep(2)
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as s:
        await s.get(url)
Jupyter notebooks and some frameworks already run an event loop. Calling
A coroutine is not a thread. It does not run independently. Until it is scheduled (wrapped in a
), it will not make progress while another coroutine is running.

async def main():
    coro = download("https://example.com")   # Not yet running
    await asyncio.sleep(5)                   # download still hasn't started
    await coro                               # Only now does it run

# Correct — create_task schedules it immediately
async def main():
    task = asyncio.create_task(download("https://example.com"))
    await asyncio.sleep(5)   # download runs concurrently during this sleep
    await task
to schedule coroutines that should start immediately
, rather than waiting until they are
ed. This allows them to make progress in the background.
For CPU-bound work inside an async program
, offload to a thread pool or process pool without blocking the event loop:

import asyncio
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor

def cpu_heavy(n):
    return sum(i * i for i in range(n))

async def main():
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    with ProcessPoolExecutor() as pool:
        result = await loop.run_in_executor(pool, cpu_heavy, 10_000_000)
    print(result)
Handle exceptions in gathered tasks explicitly.
when some tasks may fail independently and the program should process all results regardless.
on coroutines that may hang indefinitely:
to catch common mistakes like slow callbacks and un-awaited coroutines:

Coroutines are extremely lightweight. A coroutine object in Python takes on the order of a few hundred bytes of memory, compared to the megabyte-range stack of an OS thread. This means programs can comfortably manage tens of thousands of concurrent coroutines on a single machine.
The performance ceiling for async Python is largely determined by:
— Each coroutine switch involves the event loop dispatching control. This is fast (microseconds), but for extremely high-frequency, very-short tasks, the loop overhead can become measurable.
— The GIL is not an issue for async code since only one coroutine runs at a time anyway. The GIL becomes relevant only when combining async with threading.
— At scale, the actual network or disk I/O limits throughput, not the coroutine mechanism itself.
For applications needing both high concurrency
CPU-bound workloads, the standard pattern is to run multiple
event loops across multiple processes (one per CPU core), using a tool like
with multiple workers, or manually with
— The built-in event loop, task scheduler, synchronization primitives, and utilities
(Python 3.11+) — Structured concurrency for managing groups of tasks with automatic cancellation on failure
— Full-featured async HTTP client and server
— Modern async HTTP client with both sync and async APIs
— Async query builder compatible with SQLAlchemy Core
— One of the earliest async Python frameworks, now integrated with asyncio
GIL limits CPU parallelism; race conditions
Callback hell; hard to read and maintain
are the dominant model in modern Python async code, largely replacing the older callback-based patterns in
itself and in frameworks like Twisted.
A coroutine that runs a long computation without ever hitting an
will monopolize the event loop and starve all other coroutines. Always insert
in long-running loops to yield control periodically.
point inside the coroutine. If the coroutine catches
and does not re-raise it, the cancellation is silently swallowed — a reliability hazard. Always re-raise
unless there is a specific need to suppress it and clean up:
If a coroutine object is created but never awaited or scheduled, it is garbage collected without running, and Python emits a
. In long-running servers, accumulated un-awaited coroutines indicate a logic bug.
) for resources like HTTP sessions, database connections, and file handles. They guarantee proper cleanup even when exceptions or cancellations occur:

async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
    # Session is properly closed even if an exception is raised
    response = await session.get(url)

Think of the event loop as a restaurant kitchen, and coroutines as orders. A chef (the event loop) works on one order at a time. When an order needs to go in the oven (an I/O wait), the chef doesn't stand and stare at the oven — they start the next order. When the oven timer goes off, they come back to finish that dish.

No two dishes are being cooked simultaneously (no true parallelism), but the chef is never idle waiting for the oven. This is cooperative concurrency: each coroutine voluntarily pauses at defined points, allowing the "chef" to make progress on others.
keyword is the moment the coroutine says: "I need to wait for something — go do other work, and come back to me when it's ready."
expressions and resume later, without blocking the thread.
function returns a coroutine object — it does not execute it. Execution requires
keyword is the suspension point. Only awaitable objects (coroutines, Tasks, Futures) can be awaited.
Coroutines enable high-concurrency I/O on a single thread by cooperatively yielding control rather than blocking.
) inside coroutines block the entire event loop — always use async equivalents.
to run multiple coroutines concurrently and collect their results together.
Coroutines are ideal for I/O-bound work; CPU-bound work should be offloaded to process pools using
must be re-raised after cleanup to preserve the correctness of the cancellation mechanism.
